excerpt: What we want is a protocol whereby unforgeably costly bits could be created online with minimal dependence on trusted third parties, and then securely stored, transferred, and assayed with similar minimal trust. The cost has to come from outside the system itself; otherwise the system can be captured by whoever controls cost-issuance inside it.
body:
Source excerpt:
What we want is a protocol whereby unforgeably costly bits could be created online with minimal dependence on trusted third parties, and then securely stored, transferred, and assayed with similar minimal trust. The cost has to come from outside the system itself; otherwise the system can be captured by whoever controls cost-issuance inside it.

---

Cited by leaf "Why proof of work":
Proof of work is the rule that says: to extend the chain, you must show evidence that a measurable amount of real-world energy was spent doing it. The work is finding a block header whose double-SHA256 hash is below the current difficulty target — there is no shortcut, you grind random nonces until one fits [1]. The output is cheap to verify (one hash) and expensive to produce (trillions of hashes per block at current difficulty), which is exactly the asymmetry Bitcoin uses to gate writes to its ledger.

The property this buys is unforgeable costliness, in Nick Szabo's phrase. An attacker who wants to rewrite the past has to redo all the work that produced it, faster than the honest network is producing new work — that's what "51% attack" means in practice [2]. The cost is external: it shows up as electricity bills and depreciating hardware, denominated in something the protocol doesn't control. Proof of stake, by contrast, secures the chain with the same asset the chain itself issues, which makes the security cost endogenous and the slashing rules a matter of social consensus rather than physics.

The other thing PoW gets you is permissionless Sybil resistance. There is no validator set, no whitelist, no minimum balance — anyone with a power outlet and an ASIC can start contributing hashrate, and their share of block production is exactly proportional to their share of the work. That property is what makes the network resistant to capture by entities whose names you'd recognize: even a state-level adversary has to outspend everyone else in the world combined, on hardware that is itself a competitive market [3].

---

Cited by leaf "Bit Gold (Nick Szabo, 2005)":
Nick Szabo described Bit Gold in essays starting around 1998 and in a more-developed 2005 blog post. The proposal is a digital scarcity built on chained proof-of-work: a participant takes a public "challenge string" derived from the most recent solved puzzle, finds an input whose hash satisfies a difficulty target, registers the solution in a property-title registry, and the chain of solutions becomes a record of cumulative work that nobody can rewrite cheaply [1]. The framing — "unforgeably costly bits" — is Szabo's, and it is the cleanest one-line statement of what proof-of-work-backed money is actually trying to be.

The pieces Bit Gold got right and Bitcoin shares: chaining the proofs so that each one builds on the last, making the cost of rewriting history compound; using the hash output itself as the unit of value; not requiring a trusted issuer to mint or to validate; recognizing that the scarcity of the resulting tokens depends on the cost of producing them, not on any institutional commitment [2]. The piece Bit Gold left unsolved: the property-title registry. Szabo proposed a Byzantine-agreement quorum of independent servers as the registry layer, but acknowledged that designing the quorum, gating membership, and incentivizing honest behavior were open problems.

Szabo did not publish the 2005 essay in a way the Bitcoin whitepaper could cite by 2008, which is why Bit Gold is not in the references list — but the conceptual ancestry is direct enough that some readers, on reading the whitepaper, initially suspected Szabo of being Satoshi (Szabo has consistently denied this) [3]. Bit Gold is the proposal that frames the problem most cleanly; Bitcoin is the system that solved the registry-and-incentive layer Bit Gold could not.
